title: The delivery mechanism 'one shared link -> copy a Project template' does not exist on claude.ai for individual Pro accounts
attack: Blueprint section 3 sells the novice tier as 'Project template + instruction pack (zero install)' and section 4E promises 'one shared link -> copy a Project template.' claude.ai has no cross-account Project template or Project-copy feature for individual Pro subscribers (Project sharing exists only inside Team/Enterprise orgs, and the cohort is ~90% individual Pro per OQ-7). The real novice path is therefore: open claude.ai -> find Projects in the sidebar -> create a Project -> locate the 'instructions' field -> paste a multi-thousand-character instruction block from some external doc without truncating it -> separately upload a starter ledger file into Project knowledge. That is five manual steps with distinct failure modes (pasting instructions into the chat box instead of the instructions field is the classic one — the instructions then govern nothing), performed by users who, per cohort-story.md F1, 'don't use claude.ai Projects at all.' On phones and tablets — explicitly in the device mix (OQ-8) and where 'life happens' (OQ-3) — Project creation plus long-text pasting is worse still. The M1 gate ('2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided') is scored against a setup flow the blueprint has not actually designed; the phrase 'zero install' is hiding a manual install performed by the least capable actor at the moment of lowest trust, minute 1.
proposed_fix: Design the setup flow as a first-class M1 component with its own runbook: (a) distribute a single self-contained onboarding page whose step 1 is a copy-one-block button for the instructions and whose step 2 is downloading the starter ledger file, with screenshots per surface (web, mobile); (b) make the instruction pack self-verifying — its first behavior is a 'setup check' ('reply with: check my setup') that confirms instructions are active and knowledge files are present, so a mis-paste is caught in message 1, not week 2; (c) laptop-only setup explicitly recommended, phone declared review-only; (d) add 'friend completes Project setup from the link alone, no DM help' as a separate precondition inside the M1 gate, measured before the exam-kit test.

title: The ledger cannot be written on the novice tier — the core loop's persistence layer depends on a manual chore the cohort will not do
attack: The capability matrix (section 3) states the novice-tier ledger is 'Project knowledge docs, updated via commands.' Claude on claude.ai cannot write to Project knowledge — only the user can add or replace files. So every /explain, /ingest, /quiz, and /examkit that 'updates the ledger' actually ends with: Claude prints a new ledger, and the student must open Project knowledge, delete the old file, and re-upload the pasted replacement — after every session, forever. This cohort is defined by 'summarize this and stop,' vague two-line prompts, and zero workflow discipline (OQ-2, cohort-story F1/F3). They will perform this chore approximately zero times. Consequences cascade: the ledger goes stale, so the stated first-session success criterion ('the second conversation already knows their course,' cohort-story F1) silently fails; quiz sessions can't do weak-topics-first (section 4A) because mastery state never accumulates; 'weak topics steer next intake' (the loop diagram, section 2) is fiction on the tier that 100% of M1 users are on; and the ADR-0012 'self-tally line in ledger doc' usage counter dies with it, taking the M3 gate's counter data along. The blueprint never names who performs the write or how often — the single most load-bearing mechanical question of the novice tier is unaddressed.
proposed_fix: Confront the write path explicitly in the M1 design: (a) collapse all state changes into ONE end-of-session artifact ('your updated ledger — replace the file named X, takes 20 seconds') rather than per-command updates, and have the instruction pack detect a stale ledger at session start ('your ledger is from Tuesday; paste yesterday's update or say rebuild') so drift is visible instead of silent; (b) evaluate claude.ai's native memory feature as a partial substitute for hot state (weak topics, exam date), reserving the file ledger for durable artifacts; (c) degrade honestly — if the ledger is stale, quiz from Project knowledge decks directly rather than pretending mastery state exists; (d) add 'friend's ledger still current after one week of unsupervised use' to the M1 gate, since it directly tests whether F1 is actually eliminated.

title: 'Exam kit in 5 minutes' is a broken first-session promise on Pro rate limits and real materials
attack: Section 4E promises a guided first session that 'runs exam kit on the student's own course within 5 minutes.' Stack the blueprint's own facts against this: the student 'doesn't even know what to upload' (cohort-story F3), so minute 1-5 is spent hunting a syllabus and slide decks on a university portal — before any AI runs. The exam kit is the flagship heavy pipeline (materials -> triage -> decks -> countdown plan) yet it is the FIRST command a novice ever executes, on a Pro plan whose 5-hour message/context budget a semester of slide PDFs will chew through; ADR-0005 requires heavy commands to 'declare cost class and offer lighter variants,' which directly implies the full examkit should warn and downshift — contradicting the 5-minute-flagship framing. Long-conversation context limits make a single-chat semester ingest likely to stall mid-kit, producing exactly the 'why did Claude stop?' bewilderment ADR-0005 predicts, at the single highest-churn moment in the product's life. A first session that ends in a truncated half-kit converts a curious friend into a churned one, and the M1 gate is scored on precisely this flow.
proposed_fix: Redefine the first session as a 'starter kit': one course, 1-3 files (syllabus + one lecture deck), producing a visible triaged topic map + one small deck + one 3-question quiz — genuinely achievable in ~5 minutes of AI time and it demonstrates the whole loop. The full exam kit becomes the advertised session TWO ('now bring everything for your hardest exam'), chunked by design (one conversation per materials batch, ledger as the accumulator). Put a pre-flight materials checklist (the gather checklist from cohort-story F3) into the onboarding page so file-hunting happens before the chat starts, and make examkit's first output an honest time-and-quota estimate.

title: Five commands with no surface: zero discoverability affordance in an empty chat box
attack: ADR-0015 locks five commands with plain-language aliases, but on claude.ai there is no slash-menu, no autocomplete, no persistent UI — the novice faces an empty text box and must RECALL (not recognize) the alias. Recognition-over-recall is the most basic usability heuristic and the novice tier violates it structurally. The blueprint specifies exact aliases ('make my exam kit') yet the cohort's documented input style is Google-style one-liners and vague two-line prompts (OQ-2): the actual first message will be 'help me for my psych exam' or Romanian equivalents — matching no alias. Nothing in section 4 designs an intent router (which vague phrasings map to which command), a help affordance ('what can you do?'), a session-start menu, or a fallback when nothing matches. Note the aliases are also English-only as specified, while ADR-0010 mandates mirroring a Romanian-writing student — 'fa-mi kitul de examen' must trigger /examkit or the command surface simply doesn't exist for Romanian-language sessions. Without these, the 'meaningful impact on every message' requirement (ADR-0003) decays into 'meaningful impact on the messages that happen to match five English strings.'
proposed_fix: Add to the instruction pack, as testable requirements: (a) an intent-routing table mapping fuzzy phrasings (English AND Romanian) to the five commands, with a confirm-and-teach behavior ('Building your exam kit — next time you can just say make my exam kit'); (b) a standing 'help'/'what can you do' alias plus a one-line menu at the start of every NEW conversation in the Project; (c) self-advertising artifacts — every deck/ledger footer names the next command ('say quiz me to practice these'); (d) an M1 test where a friend accomplishes all four commands using only their own natural phrasings, in Romanian, with zero alias coaching.

title: Quiz-first default is scoped to the wrong commands and its bypass word is undiscoverable
attack: Critique disposition #8 already litigated whether quiz-first gets bypassed; this is a different attack: as written, the bypass is not findable and the default is applied where it reads as obstruction. Section 2 says 'answer always one word away' — WHICH word? It appears nowhere in the blueprint. A novice who asks 'explain photosynthesis' and receives a question back, without a visibly printed skip affordance, experiences a block, not a default — functionally violating the ADR-0004 amendment ('no limits... limits create friction') the owner locked. And the scope is wrong for this cohort: /explain is 'the #1 student use' conversion play (section 4A) — greeting the dominant demanded behavior with a quiz on first contact is the fastest way to teach friends 'the toolkit makes Claude worse,' the exact opposite of 'get more out of what you already pay for' (OQ-7). Bastani's guardrails lived inside a tutoring context students expected; here it ambushes a productivity ask. If early sessions habituate reflexive bypassing, the M3 bypass-rate metric gets poisoned by onboarding annoyance and can no longer test the conversion thesis it was designed to test (section 6, bet 1).
proposed_fix: (a) Scope quiz-first to retrieval-flavored contexts — /quiz, post-/ingest review offers, examkit countdown sessions — and let /explain answer FIRST, then emit items and append a one-line offer ('want me to quiz you on this in 2 questions?'); (b) mandate that every quiz-first message prints its own bypass verbatim ('say skip for the answer — no judgment'), making the one word literally on screen every time; (c) instrument bypass rate separately for week-1 sessions vs steady state so onboarding friction doesn't contaminate the thesis metric; (d) record the exact bypass token in the instruction pack and the repair runbook (ADR-0011) so it's stable across sessions.

title: The multi-course reality is undesigned: one Project or five, and what happens when materials collide
attack: Section 4A specifies 'one human-readable file per course,' but the blueprint never answers the structural question a novice hits in week 1: one Project per course, or one Project holding all courses? Both branches fail as currently designed. One-Project-per-course multiplies the already-fragile setup flow (finding 1) by 4-6 courses — a cohort that never created one Project will not create six, and instructions must be re-pasted into each. One-Project-for-everything requires course-routing logic (which ledger does this /quiz read? which course does this uploaded PDF belong to?) that appears nowhere in section 4, plus disambiguation behavior for the guaranteed novice moves: dumping a Course B PDF mid-Course-A conversation, mid-chat topic switches ('ok now quiz me for statistics'), uploading the wrong semester's syllabus, or a barely-OCR'd scan. Cohort-story F3 says wrong/incomplete inputs are a DEFINING failure of these users, and the cohort-story explicitly hands the panel the test 'does the blueprint eliminate F1-F3 for a student who changes nothing about their own behavior?' — as designed, wrong inputs flow silently into the wrong ledger and corrupt the mastery state the whole product stands on.
proposed_fix: Decide the topology now and design for it: recommend ONE Project ('Your Studies') holding per-course ledger files, since it amortizes the setup cost once. Then add to the instruction pack: (a) course detection on every upload with confirmation ('this looks like Microeconomics — file it there?'); (b) explicit course context at session and switch points ('now working on Statistics'); (c) an input-sanity check inside /ingest and /examkit (unreadable scan, duplicate file, syllabus/course mismatch -> say so instead of summarizing garbage); (d) an M1 test case where a friend with two real courses uses one Project for both and the ledgers stay uncrossed.

title: The 'guided first session' has no guide: the curriculum lives inside the thing that isn't set up yet
attack: Section 4E says onboarding 'de-programs the three observed failure patterns (OQ-2)' via a curriculum, but the only delivery vehicles named are the shared link and the Project instructions — and the instructions only act AFTER setup succeeds, while the de-programming target behaviors (one-liners, summarize-and-stop) sabotage the setup and first prompts themselves. Nothing specifies what the shared link IS (a doc? a Notion page? a chat with Flo?), and this cohort demonstrably does not read documentation — they give two-line prompts to an AI instead. Meanwhile the M1 gate word 'unaided' is in tension with 'guided first session': if Flo personally walks the two friends through it (the realistic path for a person-to-person recommended toolkit, ADR-0014), the gate measures Flo's hand-holding, not the product, and M2-M4 inherit an onboarding that never actually worked cold.
proposed_fix: Make the guide the product itself: the onboarding page contains exactly one instruction beyond setup — a copy-paste FIRST MESSAGE ('I'm new, run my first session') that triggers an in-chat guided sequence (setup check -> materials checklist -> starter kit -> 'here's what to say next time'). Define 'unaided' operationally in the M1 gate: link sent by text, no other communication until the friend reports success or gives up, with the give-up point recorded as the primary design bug to fix.

title: Phone-based review sessions are claimed in the matrix but never designed
attack: OQ-3 establishes 'claude.ai mobile matters for review sessions' and OQ-8 puts phone-primary users in the device mix, and the section 3 matrix folds mobile into the novice tier column ('claude.ai (web/mobile)') as if equivalence were free. It isn't: on mobile, quizzing from a Project works, but everything else in the loop degrades — ledger replacement (finding 2) is effectively impossible on a phone, file uploads from university portals are laptop acts, and long deck artifacts render poorly for thumb-scrolling review. The one context where spaced retrieval would actually fit the cohort's life (phone, bus, 10 minutes before seminar — 'life happens on the phone') is the context the blueprint spends zero design sentences on. Untested, the likely outcome is that mobile sessions mutate state expectations they can't fulfill (a phone /quiz can't update the ledger), reinforcing the staleness spiral of finding 2.
proposed_fix: Add a 'phone mode' behavior to the instruction pack: short-form quiz batches (5 items, one at a time), no ledger-write requests on mobile (defer with 'I'll fold this into your next laptop session — say sync ledger there'), and decks emitted in a phone-readable compact format. Include one phone-only quiz session in the M1 friend test.

title: M1 ships spaced retrieval's headline number (g=0.74) with no spacing mechanism whatsoever
attack: The design thesis (blueprint §1) sells 'the thing the learning science most rewards (spaced retrieval, g=0.74, verified)', and §2 rule 3 invokes Latimier's uniform-vs-expanding null (g=0.034 ns) to justify 'simple uniform spacing.' But Latimier's null is about the SHAPE of the schedule; it presupposes a scheduler exists. The capability matrix (§3) admits scheduled routines are impossible on the novice tier ('✗ platform limit'), and M1 (§5) is novice-tier only. So in the product that actually ships to the first cohort, no component can bring an item back after a delay — 'spacing' is the student spontaneously typing /quiz on a later day. The evidence base itself locates the core student failure in exactly this intention–behavior gap (Blasiman 2017, cited in prioritization-memo §1 Planning-Impact-4), and the cohort story says these users 'show up and study badly' with zero self-directed structure. Worse, the natural M1 flow (/ingest or /examkit emits a deck, /quiz runs on it in the same session) is massed practice: Roediger & Karpicke's verified crossover (61% vs 40% at one week, but massed WINS at 5 minutes) predicts students will score high immediately, the ledger will record that inflated score as mastery, and the product will manufacture precisely the fluency illusion it was built to destroy. Nothing in §2 or §4A specifies a minimum retention interval before an item counts toward mastery.
proposed_fix: Add a delay discipline to the ledger spec before M1: (1) items generated in a session are quizzable immediately for practice but update mastery state only from attempts ≥1 day after generation (timestamp every item; the ledger already lives in a file); (2) every ledger stores explicit next-review dates anchored backward from the exam date, and Project instructions require Claude to open each session by surfacing due items ('You have 12 items due for Bio 101 — quiz now?'), making the student's habitual act of opening a chat serve as the scheduler; (3) downgrade the §1 claim for M1 from 'spaced retrieval g=0.74' to 'retrieval practice g≈0.50, spacing dependent on session-start prompts until stage-3 routines' — and add 'median gap between generation and first quiz attempt' as an M1/M3 metric alongside bypass rate.

title: The deck emitter has no retrieval-fidelity spec — the item-quality pass filters hallucinations, not recognition items
attack: Every effect size the blueprint borrows (g≈0.50 Rowland/Adesope; g=0.74 Latimier) comes overwhelmingly from recall-format testing of studied material. The blueprint never specifies item FORMAT anywhere: §4A defines the item-quality pass as 'self-critique, duplicate/hallucination filter, user flagging' (R-11) — all accuracy criteria, zero retrieval-demand criteria. Left unspecified, an LLM's default output is multiple-choice/recognition items paraphrasing the summary it just wrote, presented in the same chat window directly below that summary. A student 'quizzing' by recognizing lures under the visible source text is doing cued re-reading, not retrieval — the literal fluency-illusion generator the lens question names. This also poisons the M1 gate: 'item-quality pass working' can pass (items accurate, deduplicated) while every item is retrieval-inert, so the gate certifies a product that fails its own theory. Disposition #5 accepted the LLM-item-quality risk but scoped it to accuracy; format fidelity was never disposed.
proposed_fix: Extend R-11 into a retrieval-fidelity spec, testable at the M1 gate: (1) default item format is cued recall (question → student produces answer) with free-recall prompts for conceptual topics; multiple-choice only where discrimination is the explicit goal, and never as majority format; (2) quiz presentation rule in Project instructions: questions delivered one at a time with answers withheld until the student responds, and never in the same message as the source summary; (3) add a fourth item-quality criterion — 'answerable only from memory, not by matching wording visible in the current context' — to the self-critique pass; (4) add the flagged reading-list item (LLM-generated question validity) as a blocking input to this spec, not a background task.

title: Calibration check — the designated defense against the verified 'can't feel the loss' harm — is itself uncalibrated: Claude grades the 'actual'
attack: The blueprint's answer to Bastani's most disturbing verified finding (harmed students didn't perceive loss; guardrailed students believed they improved when they hadn't) is one line in §4A: 'short unassisted self-test with predicted-vs-actual display.' As specified this fails on four counts. (a) The 'actual' score is graded by Claude in-chat; LLM grading of free responses is systematically lenient and sycophantic, so the instrument's denominator inflates, the predicted-vs-actual gap shrinks artificially, and the one component built to puncture the illusion of learning instead certifies it — with the authority of a 'calibration check.' (b) 'Unassisted' is unenforceable and unspecified: the self-test runs in a Project chat containing all course materials and prior explanations one scroll away. (c) Prediction timing is unspecified; judgments of learning made immediately after study are driven by processing fluency (the exact bias being measured), so an immediate global 'how will you do?' measures nothing — the literature requires delayed, item-level predictions. (d) Nothing happens after the display: no prescribed consequence when the gap is large, and the ADR-0004 no-lecturing constraint means Claude can't even editorialize — so it's a dashboard widget, and calibration-feedback research shows students don't spontaneously convert gap displays into behavior change. Bet #3 (§6) says transfer is 'watched via calibration checks' — the thesis's transfer test rests on this broken instrument.
proposed_fix: Specify the protocol: (1) item-level confidence rating (e.g., sure/unsure) collected BEFORE each answer is revealed, not one global prediction; (2) grading by answer-key self-scoring — Claude shows the model answer and the student marks right/wrong themselves (transfers the known-lenient judge role to the student against a concrete criterion, Anki-style); (3) calibration checks only on material ≥2 days old; (4) a fixed non-judgmental consequence rule: gap above threshold → affected topics auto-move to the weak list and the next /quiz opens with them — action, not commentary, which also survives the no-moralizing constraint; (5) log predicted/actual pairs in the ledger so bet #3 has actual longitudinal data rather than vibes.

title: Mastery state and weak-topic steering run on an unspecified, invalid measurement layer
attack: The ledger is 'the product's soul; everything reads/writes it' (§4A), and the loop's feedback arm is 'weak topics steer next intake' (§2). But the blueprint never defines how a quiz response becomes a mastery update: no grading rubric, no definition of a lapse, no decay of stale mastery, no distinction between an item answered correctly 5 minutes after generation and one answered after a week. Combined with LLM-lenient grading and same-session quizzing (finding 1), mastery will be systematically inflated exactly on recently-ingested material — so weak-topic steering steers AWAY from the topics most in need of delayed re-testing, and 'strong' topics exit the rotation permanently, eliminating spacing for learned items (the g=0.74 effect requires continued spaced retrieval of successes, not only remediation of failures). The M3 gate depends on this steering being meaningful; as specified it optimizes against a corrupted signal, and the human-readable ledger will confidently display wrong mastery to a cohort the evidence says cannot detect the error themselves.
proposed_fix: Write the measurement spec into the ledger design (it's a file format decision, cheap now, breaking later): each item carries {created, last_attempt, delay_of_attempt, self_graded_result, confidence}; mastery is a function only of delayed attempts (≥1 day); mastery decays toward 'due' with time since last successful delayed retrieval (a crude 7-day staleness rule beats no rule); /quiz sessions mix weak topics first BUT always include due strong-topic items (e.g., 70/30). Grade via answer-key self-scoring per finding 3.

title: §2 rule 2 overclaims Bastani: the blueprint removed the guardrail's active ingredient, then kept its causal claim
attack: Blueprint §2 rule 2 states quiz-first 'implements the only design pattern shown to eliminate AI learning harm (Bastani, verified-adjacent) without gatekeeping.' Bastani's GPT-Tutor condition eliminated harm precisely BY gatekeeping: incremental hints, full solutions withheld, teacher-built safeguards, no one-word bypass. The 'without gatekeeping' clause is the part Bastani never tested — and his own verified base rate (67% of first interactions asked directly for the answer) predicts that a cohort of one-liner askers, offered an instant unjudged bypass, habituates to bypassing within days. Disposition #3 demoted Bastani to 'verified in adjacent population' and disposition #8 logged adherence as R-12, yet the blueprint's own text still claims full mechanism coverage — the blueprint contradicts its evidence base's dispositions. The contested defense ('artifacts persist regardless of bypass') is learning-science-empty: an unopened deck in Project knowledge has zero retrieval effect; at best it is future restudy material, which Dunlosky rates low-utility. Bet #1 names bypass rate as the metric but sets no falsification threshold and no pre-committed response, which for a builder-owned project means the number can drift indefinitely without triggering redesign.
proposed_fix: Reword §2 rule 2 to 'Bastani-inspired; the tested guardrail withheld answers — our bypassable variant is an unproven adaptation whose test is the bypass metric.' Pre-commit falsification thresholds at M1: e.g., bypass >60% sustained over 2 weeks → quiz-first is not carrying learning value → trigger the R-12 response (redesign toward the deferred-assistance pattern the memo cites: one lighter retrieval touch — 'one-line guess before I answer?' — instead of a full quiz item). Also measure artifact USE (decks opened/quizzed at least once) separately from artifact existence, so the disposition-#8 defense becomes checkable.

title: Implementation intentions (d=0.65) cited for a feature that lacks the mechanism's delivery conditions
attack: §4C claims d=0.65 (Gollwitzer & Sheeran) for AI-generated if-then plans written into the ledger. The meta-analytic effect comes from participants FORMING their own if-then links to goals they hold — the mechanism is the encoding act that binds situational cue to response in the person's head. An AI-authored line ('after Tuesday's lecture → 10-min quiz on deck X') stored in a markdown file the student never re-opens creates no cue-response association; it is a note, not an implementation intention. The memo's own gaps section concedes 'implementation-intention evidence predates LLM assistants; the MOOC result may not transfer,' yet the blueprint imports the full effect size without that flag. And on the M1/novice tier there is no channel to deliver the cue (no routines until stage 3, per the §3 matrix), so the feature is doubly inert: wrong author, no trigger.
proposed_fix: Change the interaction so the student authors the plan: the tool proposes 2–3 candidate if-then slots, the student picks one and types it in their own words, and Claude echoes it back at every session start alongside due items (piggybacking on the finding-1 session-open ritual, which is the only cue channel M1 has). Strip the d=0.65 citation from the M1 feature description — attach it to the stage-3 routines where cue delivery becomes real — and log plan-followed vs plan-ignored in the ledger as the honest signal.

title: /examkit's 'what matters' triage is an unvalidated metacognitive takeover aimed at closed-book exams
attack: The flagship pipeline (§4A) converts materials into a 'triaged topic map (what matters)' for a cohort whose defining failure is that they cannot triage (OQ-1: 'not knowing what matters') and who exhibit zero verification behavior (cohort story F2). They will trust the map completely — that is its selling point. But the blueprint specifies no grounding for exam-likelihood ranking: past exams are an optional onboarding checklist item, syllabi may or may not carry weighting, and an LLM inferring 'what matters' from slide decks is guessing with confident formatting. On closed-book exams, confident misprioritization is the highest-damage failure available to this product: students narrow study to the map, the map is wrong, and the miss is unrecoverable at exam time. The F2 design obligation (claims from outside the materials are labeled) exists for content answers but is not applied to the triage layer, which is where a hallucinated priority costs the most. Nothing in the M1 gate ('2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided') tests triage accuracy — the gate measures pipeline completion, not whether the map pointed at the right topics.
proposed_fix: Require the topic map to show its evidence per topic: source signals (frequency across slides, syllabus emphasis, past-exam appearance if provided) and an explicit confidence label, with an honest banner when no past exams or weighting were provided ('ranked by coverage in your materials only — your professor may differ'). Default to coverage-complete decks with priority ORDERING rather than pruned decks, so a wrong ranking degrades efficiency, not coverage. Add a post-exam one-question retro ('did anything on the exam surprise you that the kit deprioritized?') to the M1 friend test so triage validity gets a real datapoint.

title: Self-explanation prompts (g=0.55) are opt-in offers to a summarize-and-stop cohort, with no uptake measurement and a sycophantic evaluator
attack: §4B cites Bisra 2018 (g=0.55) for self-explanation prompts 'woven into reading outputs... as an offer, not a gate.' The effect requires the learner to actually generate the explanation; the cohort's documented pattern is 'summarize this and stop' (OQ-2), so uptake of an optional 'your turn: explain X back' appended below a finished summary will predictably approach zero — and unlike quiz-first, no metric watches it, so the feature can silently contribute nothing while its effect size continues decorating the blueprint. Secondary: when a student does respond, Claude evaluates the explanation, and lenient/sycophantic evaluation ('Great explanation!') removes the corrective feedback element that makes self-explanation work.
proposed_fix: Make the prompt the default next conversational turn after /ingest (the student must decline, not opt in — same never-blocking posture as quiz-first), tally offered-vs-attempted in the ledger next to bypass rate, and give the evaluator a rubric requiring it to name one thing missing or imprecise in every student explanation before any praise.

title: The novice-tier ledger has no write path: Claude cannot update Project knowledge, so the core loop's ARTIFACT→LEDGER stage does not exist on the M1 surface
attack: Blueprint §2 makes the ledger the spine of the core loop and §3's matrix claims novice-tier 'Ledger/memory: Project knowledge docs, updated via commands.' This is technically impossible on claude.ai in 2026: Claude cannot create, update, or delete Project knowledge files. The official 'Create and edit files with Claude' help article (support.claude.com/en/articles/12111783) confirms created files are conversation downloads (or Google Drive saves) only — never written into Project knowledge; an open feature request (github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/16550) documents the actual workflow: download updated file → open project settings → delete old file → re-upload → verify. Every ledger 'update' (per §4A, triggered by every /explain, /ingest, /quiz, and calibration check) therefore requires a multi-step manual ritual from students whose defining failure (cohort-story F1) is precisely that they do zero manual persistence — they 'don't use Projects at all.' The F1 fix as designed depends on the most friction-prone behavior in the product. Cascading damage: (a) ADR-0012's 'self-tally line in ledger doc' usage counter cannot persist across chats, so the M3 gate ('bypass rate & counter data flowing from ≥5 friends') has no persistence substrate on the tier where most friends live; (b) the §6 thesis metric — quiz-first completion vs bypass rate — is unmeasurable on the novice tier; (c) §3's claim that a novice 'inherits their entire study state' when moving to the plugin only holds if the manual re-upload loop was actually followed all semester. The claude.ai memory feature (project-scoped, Pro rollout Oct 2025, support.claude.com/en/articles/11817273) softens cross-chat amnesia but is opt-in, auto-summarized, not granular mastery state, and not the user-owned markdown the blueprint promises as tier-portable.
proposed_fix: Redesign the novice-tier persistence layer before M1: (1) make 'end-of-session ledger save' an explicit, designed ritual — every command's final output ends with the updated ledger as a single copy-paste/download block plus a 3-step replace instruction, and onboarding teaches it as THE habit (mirroring how onboarding already treats Project setup as THE step); (2) require project-scoped memory enabled at onboarding as the soft backstop and design ledger updates to be memory-legible ('remember: thermodynamics weak, exam June 14'); (3) re-scope M3's bypass/counter gate to surfaces with a real write path (Claude Code plugin, Cowork), and state honestly in §3 that novice-tier ledger updates are student-executed, not command-executed; (4) evaluate Cowork (see separate finding) as the default tier for laptop-based friends, where a local ledger file CAN be auto-maintained.

title: The onboarding mechanism as specified does not exist: there are no shareable Project templates and no project sharing on Pro plans
attack: Blueprint §4E promises 'one shared link → copy a Project template' for the novice tier. On claude.ai in 2026, project sharing is a Team/Enterprise feature only (support.claude.com/en/articles/9519189); Pro users cannot invite others to a project, and there is no template, clone, export, or copy-via-link mechanism for Projects on any plan. The ~90%-Pro cohort (OQ-7) cannot receive a Project by link, period. Real onboarding is therefore: create a Project manually → paste an instruction pack (practical limit ~8,000 characters, unpublished by Anthropic) → upload starter ledger/checklist files → run first command — six-plus manual steps performed by students the cohort story describes as never having used Projects and giving 'vague, short prompts.' The M1 gate ('2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided') is directly threatened because 'unaided' setup is the step most likely to fail, and the blueprint has designed onboarding around a distribution feature that doesn't exist.
proposed_fix: Rewrite §4E around mechanisms that exist: (1) distribute the instruction pack as a public artifact or hosted page with one-tap copy blocks, sequenced as a guided 'setup chat' the student runs with vanilla Claude ('paste this, Claude will walk you through creating the Project'); (2) evaluate custom skills as the distribution vehicle — skill upload is available on all claude.ai plans including Pro (support.claude.com/en/articles/12512180, requires code execution enabled), though zips are per-account and unshareable on Pro, so the 'shared link' becomes 'download this zip, upload in Settings → Skills'; (3) count the actual setup steps and set an M1 sub-gate: a friend completes setup from the link alone, screen-recorded, in under 10 minutes.

title: RAG mode silently degrades ledger reads as course materials accumulate — the exact usage pattern /ingest and /examkit encourage
attack: The blueprint assumes the ledger is reliably in context every message ('everything reads/writes it,' §4A; weak-topic steering, §2). Official docs (support.claude.com/en/articles/11473015) state Projects auto-switch to retrieval (RAG) mode as knowledge approaches the context limit — no opt-out, no setup. Field evidence (github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/25759, Feb 2026, closed 'not planned') reports activation by FILE COUNT (~13 files at a displayed 2% capacity), with partial-fragment retrieval, missed cross-file connections, and hallucinated details — and shows the threshold changed silently between 2024 and 2026. A single course project containing ledger + syllabus + semester map + ~10 lecture PDFs plausibly crosses the threshold, after which /quiz may read a fragment of the ledger, weak-topic steering silently mis-fires, and /examkit triage hallucinates coverage — with no error signal to a novice who cannot detect it (F2: they never verify). This is also ADR-0003's own revisit trigger ('Anthropic platform changes that materially… restrict what claude.ai Projects can do') already firing before build.
proposed_fix: Add a hard design rule to §4: Project knowledge stays lean — permanently only the ledger, semester map, and topic map live there; raw materials are ingested in-chat (or in a scratch project) and their distillates written to the ledger, then the raw files are NOT added to knowledge. Cap the ledger at a stated token budget (one file, e.g. <8K tokens). Add an M1 gate test: deliberately push a test project into RAG mode and verify quiz/examkit behavior degrades detectably (Claude states what it could not retrieve) rather than silently.

title: Project instructions are treated as an enforcement layer, but adherence is probabilistic, the budget is ~8K characters, and claude.ai has no hooks
attack: §3 loads onto novice-tier project instructions: language mirroring, plan-aware behavior, artifact emission on every output, quiz-first default, five command definitions with plain-language aliases, cost-class declarations, F2 source-grounding/citation rules (cohort-story new requirement), calibration cadence, and the ADR-0012 self-tally protocol. Two platform facts break this: (1) instruction space is finite (~8,000 characters in practice; Anthropic publishes no limit) and instructions consume context every message; (2) claude.ai has no deterministic enforcement — no hooks, no post-processing — so 'rules the loop enforces by default' (§2) are really 'behaviors Claude usually exhibits until conversation length, RAG mode, or user pushback erode them.' Always-on side-tasks like 'every comprehension output also emits question items and updates the ledger' are exactly the class of standing instruction LLMs drop deep into long chats, and quiz sessions are long chats. On Claude Code, hooks CAN enforce artifact emission deterministically (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks) — meaning the two tiers don't just differ in features (§3's honest claim) but in whether the core loop's rules are enforced at all, which the blueprint never states.
proposed_fix: Treat the instruction pack as a scarce engineered artifact: budget it (character count per behavior), rank behaviors by load-bearing order (quiz-first + artifact emission + F2 grounding first), and move per-command depth out of standing instructions into command 'expansion prompts' the student triggers by alias or into claude.ai custom skills (loaded on demand, not resident every message). Add an M1 adherence test with numbers: over a scripted 15-turn session, measure emission rate, citation-labeling rate, and mirroring rate; set a pass threshold (e.g. ≥80%) and publish it in the runbook so regressions are detectable by a fresh Claude session (ADR-0011).

title: The quota paradox: the architecture maximizes per-message token cost on the shared Pro usage pool it promises to optimize
attack: ADR-0005/OQ-11 position the product as 'get more out of what you already pay for.' Platform reality: Pro usage is ONE shared pool across claude.ai, Desktop, Cowork, and Claude Code, governed by a 5-hour rolling window (~45 prompts baseline, doubled May 2026, but weighted by context size, attachments, and output length) plus weekly caps (support.claude.com/en/articles/11647753). The blueprint's design maximizes every cost driver simultaneously: materials-heavy Project knowledge re-sent each message in non-RAG mode, verbose outputs (answer + emitted deck items + ledger block per §2 rule 1), multi-artifact /examkit pipelines, long quiz conversations, and — on the power tier — 'unattended' folder ingest that can plausibly consume a full 5-hour window in one run, locking the student out of ordinary Claude during exam season. Anthropic's own usage-limit best practices (support.claude.com/en/articles/9797557) explicitly advise minimizing large project knowledge and long conversations. The predictable outcome is friends hitting walls they never hit with vanilla Claude and blaming the toolkit — inverting the adoption pitch. §4E's cost classes are messaging; the cost structure is architectural.
proposed_fix: Make quota a measured M1 gate artifact: instrument each command's typical usage weight (turns × context size) on a realistic course project and publish per-command cost classes from data, not vibes. Adopt the lean-project rule (finding 3) as the primary quota mitigation. Chunk /examkit into resumable stages with an explicit 'stop here, resume after your window resets' seam. On the power tier, rate-limit unattended ingest by design (batch caps + resume file) and schedule heavy runs at window boundaries.

title: Stage-3 routines misdescribe the platform: hooks have no clock, and scheduled tasks only fire while the laptop is awake with the app open
attack: §3 claims power-tier 'Scheduled routines… Cron/hooks: exam countdowns, daily digests.' Verified against code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks: the complete hook event list (SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, Pre/PostToolUse, Stop, etc.) contains zero time-based triggers — hooks cannot implement countdowns or digests. Actual scheduled execution options in 2026: Claude Code Desktop scheduled tasks (code.claude.com/docs/en/desktop-scheduled-tasks), which run ONLY while the desktop app is open and the machine is awake — runs are skipped, not queued, if the laptop sleeps through the slot — or OS-level cron/Task Scheduler invoking headless `claude -p`, typically with --dangerously-skip-permissions to run unattended. For a student cohort (laptops closed most of the day, OQ-3: life happens on the phone) an exam-countdown that silently skips the week before the exam is the worst possible trust failure, and Windows Task Scheduler + headless-with-permissions-bypassed machinery is a heavy ADR-0011 repair burden ('ask Claude to fix it') and a real security posture question. Also note routines burn the same shared Pro pool (finding 5): a daily digest costs quota whether or not the student reads it.
proposed_fix: Rewrite the routines row honestly and redesign around what IS deterministic: SessionStart hooks — 'whenever you open Claude Code during an exam window, the countdown/digest surfaces first' — which fire reliably and need no scheduler. Offer clock-driven delivery via Desktop scheduled tasks as explicitly best-effort ('runs when your laptop is awake'), document skip semantics in the runbook, and never build an exam-critical reminder on a fire-may-skip substrate; the student's phone calendar (an exported .ics from the semester map — trivially generatable) is the reliable countdown channel and costs zero quota.

title: The capability matrix is stale: custom skills on claude.ai and Cowork on all paid plans (GA April 2026) change which tier can actually host the product
attack: §3's matrix predates two shipped platform capabilities. (1) Custom skills are uploadable on ALL claude.ai plans including Pro (support.claude.com/en/articles/12512180 and /12512198): a skill bundle with SKILL.md + helper templates is a far more reliable command surface than cramming five command definitions into resident project instructions — it loads on demand, doesn't consume every-message context, and carries the item-quality-pass logic as files. The blueprint's novice tier ignores it. (2) Claude Cowork reached GA on all paid plans April 9, 2026, with Windows parity since Feb 2026, local folder read/write, and plugin support (support.claude.com/en/articles/13345190). For the ~90%-Pro, laptop-centered cohort, Cowork is the only zero-code surface where the ledger can be maintained automatically as a real user-owned markdown file — it dissolves the critical finding 1 for every friend willing to install the desktop app, and it runs the SAME plugin as the power tier, collapsing two codebases into one. The blueprint's matrix row for Desktop ('Same Project + local file access', routines 'limited') describes the pre-Cowork world. ADR-0003's revisit trigger — platform changes that materially expand what surfaces can do — has fired, before a line of M1 is built.
proposed_fix: Re-run the §3 capability matrix against July 2026 platform reality as a blocking pre-M1 action. Concretely evaluate a three-tier restructure: pure claude.ai Project (phone + true zero-install, honest about manual ledger saves) → Cowork + plugin (laptop default for friends: auto-maintained local ledger, real files, one command surface shared with the power tier) → Claude Code (power users, unattended pipelines). Prototype the ledger write-loop in Cowork for one course before committing M1 to the Projects-only design.

title: 'The artifact persists anyway' is false by default on the novice tier, and examkit intake hits hard file limits
attack: §2 rule 2 defends bypass tolerance with 'the artifact persists anyway,' and ADR-0009's contested critique #8 leans on the same claim ('the deck exists even if the quiz is skipped'). On claude.ai, an emitted deck exists only inside that conversation; it survives into future sessions only via the manual download/re-upload loop (finding 1) or not at all. A deck emitted in a random Tuesday chat is functionally gone. Separately, /examkit's intake meets hard platform ceilings the blueprint never mentions: 30MB per file upload/download (support.claude.com/en/articles/12111783), ~20 attachments per chat, and visual PDF analysis limited to ~100 pages — image-heavy lecture slide exports (the cohort's 'mostly digital slides/PDFs on portals,' OQ-5) routinely exceed page limits and degrade to text-only extraction, which for scanned Romanian materials with diacritics is exactly where the ADR-0010 item-quality gate will strain.
proposed_fix: Replace 'persists anyway' with a per-surface persistence definition in §2 (power tier: file on disk; novice tier: persists after the end-of-session save ritual) so the thesis metric isn't defended by a claim that's untrue on the tier being measured. Give /examkit an input preflight: count/size/page-check the uploads, split oversized decks, and tell the student what to do — vague-input-tolerant behavior (F3) applied to files, not just prompts. Add one image-heavy 100+ page Romanian slide PDF to the M1 test set.

title: The R-09 mitigation 'local-first, user-owned files' is factually false for the flagship M1 tier — ledgers live in claude.ai Projects under Anthropic's consumer training terms
attack: Critique #7's disposition (evidence base, 'Accepted → hard constraints: local-first, user-owned human-readable files') is contradicted by the blueprint's own capability matrix (§3): novice-tier ledger/memory = 'Project knowledge docs' on claude.ai — i.e., Anthropic's cloud, not local files. M1, the first shipped increment, is ENTIRELY this tier. Worse, the marginal privacy delta versus the stated competitor ('unaugmented Claude', OQ-11) is precisely the product's soul: the toolkit manufactures a NEW, concentrated performance dossier (per-course mastery state, error log, calibration failures — §4A) that would not otherwise exist, and parks it in a third party's cloud. Under Anthropic's consumer terms (updated Aug–Sep 2025, verified via Anthropic's own announcement), Free/Pro/Max chats and uploads are used for model training unless the user opts out, with 5-year retention for training-enabled data; ~90% of the cohort is on Pro (OQ-7), so this applies to essentially every first-cohort user, and a June 2026 report (techcoffeehouse) alleges the opt-out has carve-outs. The blueprint never mentions this anywhere — not in onboarding (§4E), not in the M1 gate. A privacy story that describes only the power tier while the novice tier ships first is not a privacy story; it is a liability shield made of wishful thinking. To be clear what is NOT wrong here: the students' own processing of their own data falls under the GDPR household exemption (Art. 2(2)(c)), and Anthropic — not the builder — is the controller of claude.ai data. The failure is honesty-of-record and informed use, not builder illegality.
proposed_fix: Replace the 'local-first, user-owned' claim in R-09/blueprint with an honest two-tier data-residency statement: power tier = local markdown, truly user-owned; novice tier = 'your ledger lives in your Claude account under Anthropic's consumer terms — the toolkit adds zero collection of its own.' Add one onboarding sentence (same register as the ADR-0004 integrity sentence) pointing students at the model-training privacy toggle and noting the ledger stores their performance history. Add 'training-toggle disclosure present' to the M1 gate checklist, and re-verify Anthropic's current consumer terms at build time (policy has moved twice since 2025).

title: 'Any artifact can be shared into a group chat' conflates share-safe decks with personal performance dossiers
attack: Blueprint §3 states artifact formats are identical across tiers and 'any artifact can be shared into a group chat (OQ-3, critique #11).' But §4A defines the ledger as topics + mastery state + error log + exam date, and the deck emitter/quiz session are ledger-coupled ('weak topics first'). Three concrete leak paths: (1) a student shares 'their exam kit' and the ledger — format-identical markdown, same folder, same pipeline — travels with it, disclosing their error history to the group; (2) decks emitted from ledger-aware sessions can embed weakness-ordering or ledger annotations, so even a 'deck' leaks the sharer's performance profile; (3) ADR-0012's novice-tier counter is 'a self-tally line in the ledger doc', so usage frequency data rides along with any ledger share. Once student B ingests student A's shared artifact, A's mastery/error data enters B's Project — beyond A's control, retained under B's training-toggle setting. The cohort is phone-first-social (OQ-3) and the blueprint actively encourages sharing; accidental disclosure of 'what I'm bad at, two days before the exam everyone is taking' is a genuine user harm the design invites, and no legal exemption fixes a social harm. This is a design gap, not a settled decision — critique #11's disposition only said artifacts SHOULD travel; it never examined which ones.
proposed_fix: Introduce a two-class artifact taxonomy as a design rule: SHAREABLE (decks stripped of ledger-derived annotations, topic maps, semester maps) vs PERSONAL (ledger, error log, calibration history, counter). Deck emitter's export path strips personal annotations by default; ledger files carry a first-line header ('Personal — contains your performance data; share deliberately'); move the counter tally out of the ledger doc into its own section/file. Cost: near-zero (it's prompt/template text); add 'sharing-surface check' to the item-quality pass.

title: The M4 gate silently dropped the EU AI Act half of critique #7 — and the cheap mitigation that keeps the toolkit out of Annex III is missing
attack: Critique #7 explicitly named 'GDPR / EU AI Act exposure'; the disposition and the M4 gate (§5: 'GDPR review (R-09)') retain only GDPR — the AI Act vanished from the record without a disposition, which violates the project's own defense-grade documentation standard (ADR-0001). The exposure is real but narrow: Annex III point 3(b) covers AI systems 'intended to be used to evaluate learning outcomes, including when those outcomes are used to steer the learning process' — and the toolkit's ledger literally evaluates mastery and steers the next intake ('weak topics steer next intake', §2 loop diagram). Classification, however, hinges on INTENDED PURPOSE and institutional context: regulators and industry comment (SIIA; AI Act Service Desk guidance discussions) point toward summative/institutional assessment being in scope and personal self-study/personalized-learning tools being out; additionally, Art. 2(12)'s open-source exemption applies unless the system is placed on the market AS high-risk. At M4 public release, the builder becomes a 'provider' distributing under their own name (ADR-0014 — it is literally named after him), so the intended-purpose statement is the single load-bearing artifact — and the blueprint doesn't plan one. Equally attackable in the other direction: any panel recommendation of conformity assessments, CE-marking prep, or a DPO for a free student prompt-pack would be paranoia — do not gold-plate.
proposed_fix: Add to M4 scope: (1) a one-paragraph intended-purpose statement in the README/marketplace listing — 'personal self-study tool for students' own use; not intended for use by educational institutions to evaluate students, determine access/admission, or proctor exams' — mirroring the ADR-0004 no-cheating-features language; (2) a marketing-language rule: never describe the toolkit as 'grading', 'assessing students', or instructor-facing; (3) record an explicit AI Act disposition in the evidence base so the dropped half of critique #7 is on the record. Explicitly document that no conformity assessment is undertaken and why (out of Annex III scope by intended purpose + Art. 2(12)).

title: 'GDPR review (R-09)' as an M4 gate is unfalsifiable theater under the ADR-0011 maintainer model
attack: The M4 gate says 'GDPR review (R-09)' with zero definition of what is reviewed, against what checklist, or what failure looks like. Under ADR-0011 the maintainer strategy is 'ask Claude to fix it' — so this 'formal review gate' (critique #7 disposition) will in practice be a fresh Claude session asked 'is this GDPR compliant?', which will produce plausible reassurance and pass itself. A gate that cannot fail is not a gate; this is the same failure mode ADR-0013 guards against elsewhere (quality gates must be falsifiable). Note what is NOT wrong: the M4 TIMING is correct and should be defended, not moved — during M1–M3 the users are personal friends processing their own data (household exemption for them; consent-based personal-relationship sharing toward the builder), so demanding a GDPR workup at M1 would be the paranoia error.
proposed_fix: Write the review checklist NOW (it is one page) and attach it to R-09 so the M4 gate has pass/fail content: (1) per-tier data inventory (what personal data exists, where it resides, who the controller is); (2) sharing-surface audit (finding #2's taxonomy actually implemented); (3) README privacy note ('no telemetry, no collection by the builder; novice-tier data governed by Anthropic consumer terms' + link); (4) training-toggle disclosure present in onboarding; (5) AI Act intended-purpose statement present (finding #3); (6) counter labeling accurate (finding #5). Per ADR-0011, the checklist IS the repair runbook for this gate — a fresh Claude session can execute it mechanically.

title: ADR-0012's 'anonymous' counter is not anonymous — the measurement channel is identified screenshots from named friends
attack: ADR-0012 claims 'anonymous counters' reconcile measurement with GDPR (R-09). But the actual success-metric channel (ADR-0008/ADR-0012) is 'friends voluntarily sharing counter screenshots/numbers' with the builder — usage data arriving attached to a known person's name in a chat thread is identified personal data in the builder's hands, the opposite of anonymous. This is legally fine (freely given, personal relationship, trivial data, arguably household activity) — the error is the label, and mislabeled privacy claims are exactly what a hostile reader of the public portfolio docs (success criterion 3) will quote. Secondary defect already noted in finding #2: the novice-tier tally lives inside the ledger doc, coupling usage data to the performance dossier.
proposed_fix: Rename the mechanism in ADR-0012 and the blueprint from 'anonymous counters' to 'local, voluntary self-reported counters.' Add one rule: when counter numbers are aggregated into public/portfolio docs, publish aggregates only ('4 of 6 friends weekly'), never named rows. Decouple the tally from the ledger doc (per finding #2).

title: The 'ask Claude to fix it' repair flow will routinely exfiltrate real ledgers into debug sessions — ship synthetic fixtures instead
attack: ADR-0011 requires every component be repairable by a fresh Claude session 'using only the repository', and runbooks must document 'where its state lives.' In practice, a friend whose quiz session breaks will paste their actual ledger — mastery state, error log, calibration history — into a debugging chat, or send it to the builder, because that IS where the state lives and no runbook says otherwise. Result: the builder accumulates friends' performance dossiers ad hoc (making him a de facto processor of exactly the data class R-09 worries about), and real personal data flows into additional Claude sessions under whatever training toggle that session has. Nothing in the blueprint's repair-runbook requirement (§4E, definition-of-done) addresses data hygiene during repair.
proposed_fix: Add one line to the repair-runbook template (ADR-0011 definition-of-done): every runbook ships a synthetic sample ledger/deck fixture, and the runbook's first instruction is 'reproduce with the sample fixture; only use your real ledger if the sample cannot reproduce, and strip the error-log section first.' Cost: one fake ledger file per component, generated once.

title: F1 is not eliminated: the novice-tier ledger has no write-path, so persistence collapses back into manual re-upload
attack: The cohort test (cohort-story.md) is explicit: F1 must die 'for a student who changes NOTHING about their own behavior,' and first-session success means 'the second conversation already knows their course.' The blueprint's capability matrix (§3) implements the ledger on the novice tier as 'Project knowledge docs, updated via commands' — but on claude.ai, Claude cannot write to Project knowledge. A 'command' can only emit an updated ledger into the chat; the student must then download/copy it and manually replace the Project knowledge file after every /ingest, /quiz, and /examkit. That is a recurring manual sync chore imposed on the exact population defined by never having done even one-time Project setup. The predictable outcome for a zero-behavior-change student: the ledger goes stale after session one, 'weak topics first' spaced retrieval (§4A) reads dead state, mastery tracking and exam countdown silently stop being true, and the core loop diagram (§2) is broken at its LEDGER stage on the only tier the first cohort uses. The blueprint's own soul component ('everything reads/writes it,' §4A) has no write mechanism where 100% of M1 users live. Claude.ai's project-scoped memory feature does not rescue this: the ledger schema (topics, mastery state, error log, exam date) requires structured, reliable, user-visible state, not ambient conversation-derived memory — and the blueprint never even mentions memory as the mechanism, it names knowledge docs.
proposed_fix: Redesign novice-tier persistence explicitly around what claude.ai can actually do, and document the write-path in §3: (a) make the ledger a single compact artifact that every command re-emits in full at the end of its output, with a one-line, always-identical instruction ('replace the file named LEDGER in this Project with this') so the sync action is a single rote gesture, not comprehension work; (b) evaluate project memory as a redundant carrier of the few load-bearing fields (exam dates, top weak topics) so a stale ledger degrades gracefully; (c) add 'second conversation knows the course without the student having synced anything beyond onboarding' as an explicit M1 gate criterion — that is the F1 test as written, and the current gate ('2 friends complete a real exam kit') does not test it.

title: The zero-install onboarding rests on a distribution mechanism ('one shared link → copy a Project template') that likely does not exist on individual Pro plans
attack: Blueprint §3 and §4E promise 'Project template + instruction pack (zero install)' delivered via 'one shared link → copy a Project template.' As of the platforms the blueprint itself claims honesty about (§3 'platform limit — honest about it'), claude.ai Project sharing is a Team/Enterprise-org feature; individual Pro accounts — which is what ~90% of the cohort has (ADR-0005 amendment) — have no cross-account project-copy or template-gallery mechanism. The real novice onboarding is therefore: create a Project from scratch, paste a long custom-instructions block, upload a starter ledger file, then gather and upload course materials — four-plus manual steps performed by people whose defining trait (F1 root cause) is that they have never once used Projects. The blueprint has quietly converted 'zero install' into 'manual assembly by the least-equipped users,' and nothing in §4E or the M1 gate verifies the delivery mechanism exists. If the link-to-copy mechanic is fictional, M1 fails at step zero: 'unaided' friends cannot even reach the guided first session.
proposed_fix: Before M1 build, run a 10-minute reality test: attempt to hand a Project to a second Pro account and record every step. Then rewrite §4E around the verified worst case — e.g., a single hosted setup page (or shared doc) whose entire content is copy-paste blocks in order, plus a 'setup concierge' first message the student pastes into a blank chat that walks Claude through configuring the Project with them. Add 'a friend on their own phone/laptop reaches a working Project in under N minutes with zero screen-sharing help' as an explicit M1 gate criterion, separate from exam-kit completion.

title: F2 is unaddressed: the source-grounding obligation is absent from the blueprint, and even the cohort story's version of it assumes behavior change
attack: Cohort-story.md flags the F2 fix as a '(new requirement, not yet in blueprint §4)' — and the blueprint, owner-reviewed the same day, still does not contain it. Search §4: the only anti-hallucination machinery is the item-quality pass on the deck emitter (R-11), which covers generated quiz items only. The actual F2 surface — explanations, summaries, exam-kit topic maps, i.e., the outputs the cohort consumes as course truth — ships with no source-grounding rules, no cite-where-in-your-materials behavior, no labeling of claims from outside the uploads. Worse, the cohort story's own remedy ('a standing verify-this behavior is available in plain language') fails its own test: a cohort with zero verification behavior (F2) will never invoke an available behavior; availability is behavior change. For a student who changes nothing, F2 requires verification to be performed by default inside every output, not offered. Note also the mixed-language dimension: Romanian course materials summarized in English (ADR-0010 mirroring) is a translation step that adds hallucination surface, and the Romanian quality gate (ADR-0010) currently covers quiz items, not grounding.
proposed_fix: Add a §4E cross-cutting component: 'Grounding by default' — Project/plugin instructions require that (a) claims about course material carry inline pointers to the source file/section, (b) anything not derivable from uploaded materials is visibly tagged ('outside your materials'), (c) exam-kit and ingest pipelines end with an automatic self-check pass over their own high-stakes claims (dates, definitions, formulas) rather than a student-invoked one. Make 'zero unlabeled ungrounded claims in a sampled exam kit' part of the M1 item-quality gate, and extend the Romanian pre-launch gate to grounding fidelity, not just item quality.

title: F3's root cause ('don't know what to upload') is answered with a one-time onboarding checklist, not a mechanism; the M1 gate cannot detect an exam-unfocused kit
attack: F3's root cause is wrong/incomplete inputs. The blueprint's answers are /examkit triage (§4A) and a 'gather checklist' placed in onboarding (§4E via cohort story). But onboarding happens once; exams happen per course per session, months after the checklist is forgotten — a student who changes nothing will still feed /examkit six weeks of slides and no syllabus in December. Nothing in §4 gives /examkit an input-sufficiency check: it triages whatever it receives and will confidently produce a 'triaged topic map' from an incomplete corpus — which is precisely F3 (mid, exam-unfocused output) wearing a structured costume, and arguably worse because the structure launders the incompleteness. The M1 gate compounds this: '2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided' defines completion, not exam-fit — a kit built from wrong inputs completes beautifully. The gate as written can pass while F3 survives intact.
proposed_fix: Make input sufficiency a pipeline stage inside /examkit itself: cross-check received materials against the syllabus/semester map and open with a coverage report ('syllabus lists 12 topics; your uploads cover 7 — missing X, Y, Z; proceed anyway or add files?'), with the gather checklist re-emitted at that moment, not at onboarding. Sharpen the M1 gate: the two friends' exam kits must be checked against ground truth (past exam, professor's topic list, or post-exam retrospective) for topic coverage, and at least a crude 'kit covered what the exam asked' judgment recorded.

title: The RETRIEVAL leg of the core loop has no re-entry trigger on the novice tier — the payoff stage assumes a return-and-quiz habit the cohort demonstrably lacks
attack: The loop (§2) closes only if the student comes back and runs /quiz across days (spaced retrieval is definitionally multi-session). Observed cohort behavior (OQ-2, cited in §4E) is one-liners, summarize-and-stop, copy-paste — i.e., they end sessions at the ARTIFACT stage. On the novice tier, scheduled routines are honestly marked ✗ (§3), and stage-3 countdowns arrive at M4, power tier only. So for M1–M3 novices, nothing in the design ever initiates retrieval: decks accumulate as inert markdown, and the entire 50% study-and-memory investment (ADR-0009) delivers value only to students who spontaneously adopt a new habit — the precise assumption the cohort story forbids. The conversion bet (§6.1) is acknowledged, but the blueprint hasn't pulled the one lever it already owns: ADR-0003's locked requirement of 'meaningful impact on every message' means Project instructions can inject state into every single response, yet the design leaves every-message shaping doing language mirroring and artifact emission only.
proposed_fix: Use the instruction layer as the trigger: every response in the Project ends with a one-line status footer from the ledger ('Analiza II: exam in 9 days · 4 weak topics · say "quiz me" for 5 minutes on the weakest'). This makes re-entry a zero-effort accept/ignore choice inside conversations the student was already having — consistent with ADR-0004's no-gatekeeping amendment (an offer, never a block). Track footer-accept rate at M3 alongside bypass rate; it is the honest measure of whether retrieval happens at all on the novice tier.

title: Half the cohort's assessment mix (essays) is unserved at M1, and the layer that serves essay-based closed-book exams routes to a component that isn't there
attack: OQ-4/5 and ADR-0010 record the cohort's assessment reality: essays + closed-book exams, mixed disciplines. ADR-0015 locks M1 without /feedback (settled — not contesting the command deferral). But the blueprint creates a routing contradiction it doesn't acknowledge: critique disposition #6 scopes /quiz to factual/conceptual layers and routes 'essay-level mastery to the writing-feedback engine' — which at M1 does not exist. So an essay-heavy friend at M1 gets a core loop (deck emitter + factual quiz) aimed at the layer their assessments may not test, and the designated fallback for their actual need is deferred to an unscheduled milestone. Meanwhile the success metric (ADR-0008: friends genuinely using it weekly) will be judged during exactly this window; for essay-heavy friends the honest weekly use case reverts to raw Claude, and the M1 gate can silently avoid noticing because nothing requires the two test friends to span the assessment mix — two flashcard-friendly STEM friends pass the gate and the design ships unfit for half the cohort.
proposed_fix: Two changes that respect ADR-0015's five-command ceiling: (1) put rubric-based draft feedback behavior into the M1 Project instructions as an unnamed default ('when the student shares a draft, respond with rubric critique + argument map') — ADR-0015 defers the command, not the instruction-layer behavior, and ADR-0003 requires every-message impact anyway; (2) require the M1 gate's two friends to span the assessment mix (at least one essay-heavy/humanities), and have /quiz emit essay-appropriate retrieval formats (argument reconstruction, 'outline the answer to this past-exam question') for courses the ledger marks as essay-assessed.

title: Phone-first cohort, laptop-shaped product: every M1 flow degrades or breaks on the device where the cohort actually lives
attack: OQ-3/Round A establish phone-first socials, and §3 lists 'claude.ai (web/mobile)' as the novice surface — but every concrete M1 flow is desktop-shaped: multi-file drag into Project knowledge (§3 intake), the ledger download-edit-reupload round-trip (finding 1, strictly worse on a phone), a 'guided first session' involving template copying and file gathering (§4E), and consumption of 'plain markdown decks' (§3). The social-distribution answer to critique #11 — 'any artifact can be shared into a group chat' — is markdown files: on WhatsApp/Messenger these render as raw pipes-and-hashes text or as an unopenable .md attachment. The blueprint's own virality mechanism is illegible on the cohort's actual medium. Nothing in the M1 gate is run on a phone, so the design can pass all gates having never been touched on the device through which recommendations, sharing, and half of usage will flow.
proposed_fix: Declare a supported-device posture per flow in §3: intake and setup are laptop moments (say so in onboarding: 'do this once, at a laptop'), while /quiz and shared artifacts must be phone-native. Concretely: quiz sessions must work as pure conversational turns with zero file access; shared artifacts get a 'chat-paste format' (clean plain-text rendering, no markdown tables) emitted alongside the canonical .md; and add 'one friend completes a quiz session and shares one artifact entirely from their phone' to the M1 gate.

title: The thesis-testing metric is collected via a mechanism that presupposes the cured behavior: self-tallied bypass data on the novice tier is survivorship-biased by construction
attack: ADR-0009 names quiz-first completion vs bypass rate 'the single thesis-testing metric,' and §3 implements it on the novice tier as a 'self-tally line in ledger doc.' Chain the design's own facts: (a) the ledger on claude.ai requires a manual student-performed sync (finding 1); (b) F1 says this cohort maintains no persistence at all; (c) the students most likely to bypass quizzes are exactly the students least likely to perform ledger hygiene. Therefore the counter data that reaches M3's gate ('bypass & counter data flowing from ≥5 friends') is drawn overwhelmingly from the compliant tail — the metric will systematically understate bypass, i.e., bias in the flattering direction on the one number the conversion bet (§6.1) lives or dies by. ADR-0012 (local counters, voluntary sharing — locked) is not the problem; the novice-tier implementation of it is: a tally that only exists if the user does bookkeeping is not a measurement, it's a diary.
proposed_fix: Keep ADR-0012's local/voluntary constraints but move the tally out of student hands: Claude maintains the counter inside the ledger block it re-emits every session (students transport it by the same single sync gesture, never author it), and every quiz offer/accept/bypass is stamped there automatically. For M1–M2, additionally treat the metric as qualitative: a 10-minute debrief with each gate friend ('show me your last three chats') counts observed bypasses directly. Flag in §6.1 that novice-tier bypass numbers carry a known compliant-user bias until M3 plugin data exists.

title: The novice-tier ledger write path does not exist as described — claude.ai cannot update Project knowledge
attack: Blueprint §3 capability matrix says novice-tier ledger/memory is 'Project knowledge docs, updated via commands.' This is not a real platform capability: Claude in a claude.ai conversation cannot write, edit, or replace files in Project knowledge. The only honest write path is: command emits an updated ledger in-chat → student downloads it → manually deletes the old file → re-uploads the new one — every single session. The blueprint's own core loop (§2, INTAKE→ARTIFACT→LEDGER→RETRIEVAL) and cohort-story F1 ('the second conversation already knows their course', for a student 'who changes nothing about their own behavior') both depend on a persistence layer the launch tier cannot maintain automatically. The very cohort being served is defined by NOT doing manual file hygiene (F1: they re-upload docs per conversation because Projects setup is too much). claude.ai's project-scoped memory feature could paper over this, but it is opaque, not exportable, not schema-controlled, and contradicts §3's 'identical human-readable markdown artifacts' claim and the R-09 local-first/user-owned-files posture. As designed, the LEDGER stage — 'the product's soul' (§4A) — silently fails on the tier that ships first at M1, and neither the student nor the non-technical owner will see it fail: sessions just quietly stop knowing the course.
proposed_fix: Redesign the novice-tier ledger contract explicitly before M1 build: (a) specify the manual round-trip as a first-class, ritualized step — every command ends by emitting the full updated ledger as a downloadable artifact plus a one-line instruction ('replace course-X-ledger.md in Project knowledge now'), and onboarding drills this once; (b) make staleness self-announcing — the ledger carries a 'last updated' header and session count, and Project instructions require Claude to open every session by stating ledger age and flagging drift ('this ledger is 3 sessions old — paste or re-upload the latest'); (c) decide and document the claude.ai memory question now (use it as a redundant cache, or explicitly forbid reliance on it) in the ledger runbook; (d) test the full two-session persistence loop with a real novice as part of the M1 gate.

title: LLM full-rewrite ledger has no schema, no validator, and no recovery path — corruption is monotonic and invisible
attack: The course ledger (§4A) is rewritten by an LLM on effectively every interaction, on both tiers. Each rewrite conditions only on the previous rewrite — there is no external ground truth — so errors compound monotonically: dropped error-log entries, hallucinated mastery states, mangled exam dates, reordered/renamed topics, format drift, silent truncation when the file gets long. After 50 updates the ledger is a game of telephone with itself. The blueprint ships an item-quality pass for decks (R-11) but nothing analogous for the ledger, which is strictly more load-bearing: quiz weak-topic steering (§4A), plan-aware behavior (§4E, plan stored in ledger), calibration data, exam dates, and the ADR-0012 usage counter all read from it. ADR-0011's runbook spec ('where its state lives, how to test a fix') is unsatisfiable here because there is no defined canonical state to test against — a fresh repair-session Claude looking at a corrupted ledger cannot distinguish corruption from legitimate content. The non-technical owner has zero fsck: a ledger that says mastery of topic X is 'strong' when the entry was hallucinated four rewrites ago produces bad quiz steering that looks exactly like working software.
proposed_fix: Make the ledger a specified, checkable format, not free prose: (a) publish a canonical ledger schema doc in the repo (required sections, key names, date formats, worked example) that both tiers' instructions reference verbatim; (b) make high-loss sections append-only (error log, session history, counter) so rewrites add lines rather than regenerate them; (c) ship a 'ledger health check' command — Claude validates the student's ledger against the schema doc and reports anomalies in plain language — and make it the first step of every repair runbook; (d) document a rebuild-from-artifacts recovery path (decks + syllabus + semester map are sufficient to reconstruct a minimal ledger) so corruption is survivable, not fatal; (e) instruct periodic snapshots (power tier: git commits already; novice tier: 'keep last week's ledger file until the new one works').

title: The repair-runbook model does not fit a tier whose 'code' is prose instructions with no version, no telemetry, and no update channel
attack: ADR-0011 and §4E assume component→runbook→fresh-Claude-repairs. That model works for plugin scripts (deterministic failure, reproducible locally). It structurally fails for the M1 Project template: (1) failures are behavioral and probabilistic — Claude 'stops emitting deck items sometimes' or 'drifts out of quiz-first' — with nothing to reproduce; (2) the repair session cannot see the deployed instance — the student's Project, their possibly-truncated copy of the instructions (copy-paste loses text; Project instructions have length limits that students can silently exceed as the pack grows), or their conversation history; (3) there is no version pinning — once ten friends copy the template, fixing the repo fixes nobody; the installed base forks from the repo on day one, and the runbook describes a template no user is actually running; (4) the failure-report path is 'friend tells Flo something feels off' — a signal too vague for any runbook to consume. The M2 gate ('fresh-session Claude can repair each component from runbooks alone') tests exactly the tier where runbooks work and skips the tier where they don't.
proposed_fix: Treat the template as versioned, self-describing software: (a) embed a version string in the Project instructions AND stamp it into every emitted artifact's header, so any pasted-back artifact identifies the installed version; (b) ship a '/checkup' self-diagnostic (plain-language alias: 'is my toolkit working?') that makes Claude run through a fixed checklist — instructions present and complete, ledger parses, deck format valid, language mirroring active — and emit a short report the student can paste to Flo, giving repair sessions a structured input instead of vibes; (c) write a documented update procedure ('how to update your Project in 2 minutes') and a change-announcement habit (the group chat the cohort already lives in, per OQ-3); (d) commit golden transcripts (example correct sessions per command) to the repo as behavioral fixtures the repair session can diff against.

title: Platform and model changes have a named revisit trigger but no detection mechanism — the canary is a confused friend
attack: ADR-0003's revisit trigger is 'Anthropic platform changes that materially expand or restrict what claude.ai Projects / custom instructions can do' — but nothing in the blueprint observes that trigger. The novice tier is built entirely on surfaces Anthropic changes routinely: Project instructions handling, project knowledge retrieval/chunking, memory behavior, and above all silent model upgrades that shift instruction-following (quiz-first adherence, artifact formatting fidelity, language mirroring are all instruction-following behaviors).
