When a model bump degrades these, nothing errors — output quality just shifts, and the first detection is a friend vaguely noticing weeks later, filtered through the F2 cohort trait of not verifying anything. Worse, the repair strategy is circular: 'ask a fresh Claude to fix it' means asking the changed model to diagnose its own drift, with no recorded baseline of what correct behavior looked like. The blueprint already commits to a quarterly incumbent scan (R-10) but not to scanning the platform its own product stands on — which is the more likely source of breakage.
proposed_fix: Add a documented smoke-test ritual as a cross-cutting §4E component: a fixed 'golden session' script (5–8 test prompts with expected behaviors per command: does /ingest emit deck items, does the ledger update render, does Romanian input get mirrored) that the owner runs monthly and after any announced platform/model change, logging pass/fail in the repo. Record the model/date each runbook was last verified against. Piggyback the cadence on the existing quarterly incumbent scan so it costs one extra hour, not a new process.

title: 'Identical artifact formats across tiers' is asserted, never specified — the tiers will drift with every plugin release
attack: §3 stakes the graduation path ('a novice who later installs the plugin inherits their entire study state') on identical plain-markdown formats, but no canonical format specification exists anywhere in the blueprint — the format is implicitly whatever each tier's prose instructions cause Claude to emit that day. Two independent drift forces guarantee divergence: (1) the plugin will accumulate machine-read metadata (spacing schedule fields, counters, version stamps, front-matter) because skills and hooks need structured input, while the novice tier's prose-instruction emissions reproduce structure only approximately and lag every plugin change; (2) ADR-0010 language mirroring means a Romanian student's sessions can rewrite ledger section headings and deck fields in Romanian, after which the plugin's parsers — and any English-session Claude — face a mixed-language schema. Nobody ever tests the round trip: no milestone gate (§5) loads a novice-produced ledger into the plugin or vice versa. The 'inheritance' claim also assumes the novice's artifacts are all collected somewhere — but deck items emitted mid-conversation live in chat scrollback unless the student saved them, so the 'entire study state' may be mostly unrecoverable at graduation time.
proposed_fix: Promote the artifact format to a first-class component with its own file and runbook: one canonical spec doc (ledger + deck schemas, worked examples, rule that structural keys/headings are always English while content mirrors the student's language) referenced by both the Project instructions and the plugin. Add a cross-tier round-trip test to every milestone gate from M2 onward: novice-produced ledger loads into the plugin unmodified, plugin ledger works when pasted into a Project. Specify in the novice instructions where each artifact must be saved (which file, what name) so 'entire study state' is a defined set of files, not a hope.

title: The self-tally usage counter is unauditable fiction, and two milestone gates plus success criterion #1 stand on it
attack: ADR-0012 and §3 implement novice-tier measurement as 'a self-tally line in ledger doc' — an integer an LLM is asked to increment during full-file rewrites. This is the most corruptible state design available: LLMs miscount, rewrites clobber or reset the line, and the manual re-upload loop (finding 1) means sessions that never got saved never got counted. Corruption is undetectable by construction — nobody can distinguish 'invocations: 137' from a hallucinated number, and the non-technical owner has no way to audit it. Yet this number is load-bearing: ADR-0008 success criterion #1 (weekly friend usage) is measured by friends sharing these counters, and the M3 gate requires 'bypass rate & counter data flowing from ≥5 friends.' The single thesis-testing metric of the whole project (quiz-first completion vs bypass rate, ADR-0009) inherits the same defect: it is presumably tallied by the same mechanism, so the number that decides whether the conversion bet lives or dies is produced by the least reliable component in the system.
proposed_fix: Never store a running integer; store events. Replace the tally line with an append-only usage log section: one line per command invocation (date · command · quiz-completed/bypassed), appended, never rewritten. The count is derived by counting lines — recountable, auditable, and corruption shows up as visibly malformed lines instead of a silently wrong number. This costs nothing extra on either tier and makes the M3 gate's bypass-rate data actually trustworthy.

title: M1 has no repairability or persistence gate — the tier with the most invisible failure modes ships with the least verification
attack: §4E declares repairability review 'part of definition-of-done' for every component, and ADR-0011 is the load-bearing maintainer model — but the §5 milestone table applies a repair gate only at M2, to the plugin. M1, the novice-tier template, gates on '2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided; item-quality pass working' — a one-shot, single-session pipeline test. It never tests the persistence loop that is the actual thesis (F1: the second conversation knows your course), and never tests whether anything about the template is repairable. This inverts the risk: the novice tier is precisely where failures are invisible (no error messages, only quality degradation), where users are least able to report ('it feels dumber?'), and where the owner has no debugging access to deployed instances. A blueprint could pass M1 as written while the ledger loop, the counter, and the runbooks are all non-functional — and per the cohort story's own challenge ('does the blueprint eliminate F1–F3 for a student who changes nothing?'), F1 would remain unsolved undetected.
proposed_fix: Extend the M1 gate with two cheap checks: (a) a persistence check — at least one of the two friends returns for a second session on a different day and Claude demonstrably knows their course and quiz history without re-uploading materials; (b) a break-and-repair drill — deliberately corrupt one ledger and mangle one instruction block, hand each to a fresh Claude session armed only with the repo runbooks, and require successful diagnosis and recovery. If (b) is 'too early for M1', that is an admission the runbooks are decorative until M2 while real friends run the software.

title: Language mirroring writes into shared state — ADR-0010 and the ledger schema are on a collision course
attack: ADR-0010 mandates always mirroring the student's language with zero configuration, and the Romanian cohort will mix languages across sessions (OQ-5b: 'English-first in mind, but mirror'). Nothing in the blueprint separates conversational language from state-file language, so a Romanian session will plausibly rewrite ledger headings, mastery labels, and deck field names in Romanian; the next English session, or the plugin's parser, then meets a bilingual schema. This is a slow, silent corruption vector that a fresh repair-session Claude will misread as legitimate content, and it directly undermines the cross-tier format identity of §3. The Romanian item-quality gate (ADR-0010 consequence) tests output quality, not state-file integrity — the wrong test for this failure.
proposed_fix: One rule, added to the artifact format spec and both tiers' instructions: structural elements of state files (section headings, field keys, status labels, dates) are always English; free-text content (topic names as the course names them, explanations, error notes) mirrors the student. Add a bilingual ledger round-trip case (Romanian session updates → English session reads) to the Romanian pre-launch gate.

title: The habit loop has no cue: the novice tier ships with zero re-engagement mechanism, making the #1 success criterion (weekly use) structurally unreachable
attack: Success criterion #1 (ADR-0008) is friends *genuinely using it weekly*. Behaviorally, weekly use requires a recurring trigger. The blueprint's own capability matrix (§3) marks scheduled routines on the novice tier as '✗ (platform limit — honest about it)', and routines only arrive at M4, power tier — a tier the novice friends will never install. Nothing substitutes for the missing cue: no calendar artifact, no self-set phone reminder step in onboarding, no social loop. The core loop diagram (§2) shows 'weak topics steer next intake', but every arrow in that loop presupposes the student has *already opened the Project unprompted*. The if-then plans (§4C, Gollwitzer d=0.65) cannot serve as the cue in their current form: implementation-intention effects come from self-generated, rehearsed if-then statements anchored to a concrete situational trigger — a plan text sitting inside a ledger file in a Project the student hasn't opened is inert by definition. Meanwhile the flagship command (`/examkit`) is intrinsically episodic — Romanian exam sessions cluster in Jan/Feb and May/Jun — so the natural usage shape is exam-time bursts with month-long dead zones, exactly the drift pattern the weekly metric will read as churn. Between M1 (novice template) and M4 (routines), a span with no calendar bound (ADR-0013), there is no mechanism anywhere in the design that produces a *second session*.
proposed_fix: Add a 'cue layer' to M1 scope, entirely within novice-tier platform limits: (1) `/examkit`'s countdown plan emits a phone-importable calendar file (.ics) or a paste-ready checklist of dated 10-minute quiz blocks — the student's own phone becomes the scheduler; (2) onboarding includes a mandatory step where the student *speaks aloud and sets* one if-then plan anchored to an existing weekly event ('after Tuesday's lecture → open the Project, say quiz me') and sets the corresponding recurring phone reminder themselves; (3) design shared-deck artifacts (critique #11 disposition) as reciprocal cues — a deck arriving in the group chat is a trigger for the receiver. Add to the M1 gate: at least one friend returns for an unprompted second session within 7 days.

title: The ledger — 'the product's soul' — depends on a janitorial habit no novice will sustain: claude.ai cannot autonomously update Project knowledge
attack: §3 says the novice-tier ledger is 'Project knowledge docs, updated via commands', and §4A calls the ledger 'the product's soul; everything reads/writes it.' But on claude.ai, Claude does not write to Project knowledge; at best it outputs an updated ledger document that the *student* must manually re-upload/replace in Project knowledge after every session. That means every study session ends with unrewarded homework — precisely the curation chore this cohort has already demonstrated it won't do (cohort-story F1: they don't even use Projects; they re-upload the same PDFs per chat). The failure cascade is direct: skipped paste → stale ledger → weak-topic steering (§2 loop, M3) serves wrong quizzes → retrieval feels irrelevant → churn. Worse, OQ-3 designates the *phone* as the review-session surface, and editing Project knowledge on mobile is maximally painful — so the surface designated for the retention behavior is the one that cannot close the loop. The blueprint never names this write-path problem, and the M1 gate ('2 friends complete a real exam kit unaided') can pass in one sitting while the ledger goes stale by session three.
proposed_fix: Treat ledger persistence as a first-class M1 design problem: (1) verify what project-scoped memory on claude.ai Pro can carry automatically and lean on it before Project-knowledge files; (2) if manual replacement is unavoidable, make it a single atomic artifact with an explicit, celebrated 30-second 'save your progress' ritual at session end (framed as reward/investment, not chore), never required on mobile — mobile quiz sessions batch their ledger delta into the next laptop session; (3) add to the M1 gate: 'ledger reflects reality after 3 real sessions across 2 surfaces without owner intervention.'

title: Quiz-first, as specified, actively trains its own bypass: friction asymmetry plus habituation turns R-12 from a risk into a near-certainty
attack: This goes beyond the already-disposed critique #8 (artifacts persist on bypass). §2 rule 2 puts the quiz-first default in the path of the #1 *demanded* behavior — explanations — at exactly the moments of need when novices show up (stressed, goal = get the answer). Behaviorally, each bypass is a rehearsal: a one-word escape, executed under motivation to escape, immediately rewarded with the thing the student actually wanted. That is a textbook habit-formation gradient — within days 'skip' becomes automatic, issued before the quiz item is even read. After bypass automatizes, the product is behaviorally indistinguishable from raw Claude plus silently accumulating deck debt (see the review-debt finding). The blueprint's defense that 'the artifact persists anyway' conflates persistence with value: a deck that is never retrieved carries none of the g=0.74 effect the thesis rests on (§1). §6 bet 1 correctly names bypass rate as the thesis-testing number, but the design contains no adherence architecture whatsoever to keep that number low — the Bastani result the pattern borrows came from a *structured tutoring context*, not from interposing quizzes on demand-driven Q&A.
proposed_fix: Re-scope quiz-first by context: (1) in demand-driven Q&A (`/explain`, ad-hoc questions), never interpose — instead *append* exactly one atomic retrieval item (<15 seconds, answerable inline) after the answer, when motivation is already satisfied; (2) reserve true quiz-first for declared review contexts (`/quiz`, countdown-plan sessions); (3) equalize friction — answering the one item and skipping it must both cost one short reply — and reward compliance instantly (a visible mastery tick). Instrument bypass separately per context so the M3 metric can tell 'skipped a quiz session' from 'refused an appended item.'

title: The loop has no reward: retrieval as designed is a stream of punishment (weak-topics-first failure density, calibration humiliation, no completion moment)
attack: Walk the loop (§2) as an experience: `/quiz` serves 'weak topics first' (§4A) — i.e., the session is deliberately loaded with the items the student is most likely to get wrong, so the modal in-session experience is failure. The difficulty literature puts optimal training success around ~85% (Wilson et al. 2019, 'Eighty Five Percent Rule'); weak-first sampling drives success far below that, which is where frustration and avoidance live. Calibration checks (§2 rule 4, §4A) then periodically inform a demonstrably overconfident student that they are worse than they believe — a repeated negative-affect event with no specified positive framing. And nowhere in §4 is any completion moment, progress display, or session-end payoff specified; 'mastery state' lives as text in a ledger file. Cue exists nowhere (finding 1), and reward exists nowhere: two of the three habit-loop legs are absent from a design whose #1 success criterion is a retained habit. The blueprint knows this is a gap — the spaced-repetition dropout / desirable-difficulties-abandonment literature is explicitly parked on the unread reading list (evidence base, standing obligation 4) — yet M1 ships the aversive behavior before that reading happens.
proposed_fix: Specify the reward architecture as an M1 deliverable, not backlog: (1) interleave weak items with near-mastered items to hold in-session success near 80%; (2) end every quiz with a concrete progress delta ('2 topics moved shaky → solid; exam readiness 61% → 66%') rendered as a small visual artifact the student can screenshot straight into the group chat (making the reward double as the ADR-0012 sharing mechanism); (3) reframe calibration output as a skill trending upward ('your prediction accuracy improved 12% since last check'), never as raw overconfidence exposure. Pull the dropout literature review forward to pre-M1.

title: Auto-emitting items from every output builds a review-debt spiral with no relief valve — the canonical Anki abandonment pattern
attack: §2 rule 1 makes every summary and explanation emit retrieval items, and §2 rule 3 fixes uniform spacing. Nothing in §4 specifies item retirement, a per-session review budget, a cap on items emitted per ingest, or any backlog-forgiveness mechanism. A student who ingests a semester's slides accumulates hundreds of due items within weeks; the due-pile becomes a guilt object; guilt-avoidance generalizes from the pile to the product. This is the best-documented failure mode of spaced-repetition systems in the wild — adherence, not efficacy, is the binding constraint in the med-student SRS literature, and the blueprint's own reading list admits the dropout literature is unexamined. The irony: the harder the intake funnel (§4B, 25% effort) succeeds, the faster the retrieval core drowns. For this cohort specifically — whose stated failure is *triage* (OQ-1, 'not knowing what matters') — handing back an untriaged mountain of auto-generated cards reproduces their original failure inside the product.
proposed_fix: Add deck hygiene to the deck emitter's item-quality pass (R-11): (1) cap emitted items per ingest, ranked by the same exam-likelihood triage `/examkit` already performs — items inherit a priority tier; (2) fixed session budget (default ~10 minutes / ~12 items) with 'due' never displayed as a raw count; (3) automatic retirement of repeatedly-mastered items; (4) a one-command backlog amnesty ('reset my reviews to what matters for the next exam') that reframes falling behind as re-triage, not failure.

title: The design intervenes only inside an idealized planned session; it has nothing for the cohort's #1 stated failure (distraction/phone) and no compressed-runway exam mode
attack: OQ-1's answer ranks 'distraction & phone' first among exam-prep failures, and the discovery note explicitly shifts design weight toward *in-session structure*. Yet §4 contains no session container at all: no timeboxed unit, no defined session length, no structure that bounds a study block against drift — the 10-minute quiz block appears only as an example string inside if-then plans (§4C). The product as designed is a set of commands, not a session; the moment-of-failure (attention collapse mid-session, or panic the night before) is exactly when a student won't compose commands. Relatedly, `/examkit` (§4A) outputs a *countdown plan* — an artifact that presupposes days of runway. The cohort story says they don't start too late, but 'exam tomorrow, 6 hours, which third of this material do I cram' is still a moment this cohort will hit, and it is the single highest-leverage moment for word-of-mouth ('it saved me the night before X'). The blueprint has no answer for it; a countdown plan generated at 11pm for an 8am exam is noise.
proposed_fix: Two additions: (1) make the bounded session a first-class object — 'quiz me' opens a declared block (default 10 minutes, N items, visible progress, hard stop with the progress-delta reward), so the product supplies the in-session structure the cohort lacks; (2) add a compressed-runway branch to `/examkit`: when the exam is <48h away, skip the countdown plan and emit a triaged cram sequence (highest-exam-likelihood topics, rapid-fire retrieval with answers one word away, hourly checkpoints). Same components, re-sequenced for the panic context.

title: Entry-point habit competition: friends' existing reflex is 'new chat', which routes them AWAY from the Project — the 'intercept existing behavior' thesis fails at the UI level
attack: §1's thesis is interception: 'intercepts the behavior students already exhibit, on the tool they already pay for.' But the behavior they exhibit (cohort-story F1) is opening a fresh claude.ai conversation — which on claude.ai's default UI is *outside* any Project. Every habitual slip into a plain chat means zero toolkit instructions apply, no artifact is emitted, the ledger isn't consulted, and F1 (context loss) recurs invisibly — the student experiences 'the toolkit' as inconsistently magical, which corrodes trust faster than consistent absence would. The design shapes behavior only after the student is inside the Project, but installs no habit for getting inside it; onboarding (§4E) covers copying the template and a first `/examkit` run, not the entry reflex. This is the 'action' leg of the habit loop failing on pure navigation friction, and it silently corrupts the thesis metric too: bypass/usage tallies in the ledger can only see in-Project sessions, so out-of-Project drift is unmeasured by design.
proposed_fix: Make entry-habit installation an explicit onboarding outcome: student pins/bookmarks the Project on laptop and locates it in the mobile app during the guided session; onboarding ends with the rule stated as an if-then ('anything about uni → start in the Project'); mirror language ADR-0010 style, zero config. Add an M1 gate observation with the two test friends: what fraction of their university-related chats over one week actually start in the Project — if it's low, entry friction, not feature quality, is the first thing to fix.

title: The measurement design is blind to churn: voluntary counter-sharing samples only the retained, so success criterion #1 and the bypass metric cannot register the failure they exist to detect
attack: Within the locked ADR-0012 constraints (local counters, voluntary sharing), the behavioral selection effect is stark: a friend who drifts away — the precise event success criterion #1 guards against — stops opening the Project, stops accruing counters, and has no occasion or motivation to share a screenshot of non-use. M3's gate ('bypass rate & counter data flowing from ≥5 friends') will therefore be populated exclusively by engaged users; measured bypass rate and weekly-use figures carry survivorship bias in the optimistic direction, and §6 bet 1's 'single thesis-testing number' inherits it. Additionally, 'weekly' is the wrong shape for an exam-cyclical cohort — healthy use may legitimately be bursty (heavy in session, light mid-semester), and the metric as stated can't distinguish burst-and-return from churn.
proposed_fix: Keep ADR-0012 intact and exploit the cohort's actual structure — these are the owner's friends: (1) schedule a standing owner-initiated fortnightly ask ('screenshot your counter?') to all onboarded friends, and pre-register non-response as churn, not missing data; (2) define the retention metric as 'active in N of the last 4 weeks, adjusted for exam calendar' rather than raw weekly; (3) have the ledger tally record last-session date so any shared screenshot also reveals gaps, giving a cheap re-engagement conversation hook ('saw you stopped after the deadline — what broke?').

title: Design thesis opens with a claim the project's own findings contradict: '#1 and #2 uses in every survey'
attack: Blueprint §1 states explanations and summaries are 'the #1 and #2 uses in every survey (HEPI 2025/26, verified)' and §4A repeats 'the #1 student use in every survey.' The verified record only supports this for the UK *assessed-work* task ranking (HEPI 2025: explain 58%, summarise 48%; HEPI 2026: 61%/49% — both verified in findings.json). The same findings.json falsifies the universal form: the verified HEPI 2026 *general-use* ranking puts text generation #1 at 56% with summarising textbooks #2 at 38%; DEC 2024 (N=3,839, 16 countries) puts information search #1 at 69% with summarising mid-tier at 33%; and Anthropic 2025 observed usage shows ~47% Direct answer-seeking with Understanding at 10% and Remembering at 1.8%. The prioritization memo is careful ('the #1 assessment use everywhere it's measured'); the blueprint silently dropped the qualifier. A hostile examiner armed with the project's own evidence base sinks the thesis's first sentence.
proposed_fix: Restore the memo's qualifier in §1 and §4A: 'the top two assessed-work uses in the UK series (HEPI 2025/26, verified) and the #1 comprehension use in the German and Chegg surveys.' The verified citations then fully support the sentence; nothing else in the thesis needs to change.

title: Bastani cited as 'verified' in §2 rule 4, contradicting §2 rule 2, ADR-0009's demotion, and the verifier's own correction
attack: Internal inconsistency: §2 rule 2 correctly labels Bastani 'verified-adjacent' (per critique #3, accepted in ADR-0009: 'demoted from verified causal to verified in adjacent population' — Turkish high-school math), but §2 rule 4 cites the same RCT as '(Bastani, verified)' with the wording 'students demonstrably cannot feel AI-induced loss.' That wording restates the exact overreach the project's own verifier corrected in findings.json: 'the headline "Students cannot feel AI-induced learning loss" universalizes ("cannot") what the paper reports as an observed non-perception in this single RCT's self-report measures.' The blueprint thus contradicts both its own rule 2 and a locked ADR amendment, and does so on the claim justifying the calibration-check component.
proposed_fix: Label both Bastani uses 'verified-adjacent' and reword rule 4 to the verified form: 'harmed students did not perceive worse performance in the one RCT that measured it (Bastani, verified-adjacent) — calibration checks make the loss visible and simultaneously test whether the pattern holds in our cohort (bet 3).'

title: 'Implements the only design pattern shown to eliminate AI learning harm' misattributes Bastani to a variant Bastani never tested
attack: §2 rule 2 claims quiz-first-with-instant-bypass 'implements' the Bastani guardrail. Per the verified findings.json record, Bastani's GPT Tutor was: teacher-designed hints, correct solutions embedded in the prompt, and explicit instructions NOT to reveal answers — the answer was never 'one word away,' and there was no bypass. The blueprint's deviation (instant unjudged answer access) is precisely the load-bearing feature, so 'implements' claims equivalence the source cannot support; note also GPT Tutor produced no positive effect vs control, only harm elimination. The nearest evidence for a bypassable variant — the deferred-assistance study (arXiv 2026, N=97 grad students, rated moderate, unverified) — is cited in the memo (§4.3) but absent from the blueprint. This is distinct from disposed critique #8 (adherence/R-12): the issue here is citation fidelity, not whether students will bypass.
proposed_fix: Reword rule 2: 'adapts the guardrail principle behind the only pattern shown to eliminate AI learning harm (Bastani, verified-adjacent); the bypassable variant we ship is directly supported only by moderate-strength deferred-assistance evidence (arXiv 2026) and is itself under test via the bypass-rate metric (R-12).' Add the deferred-assistance citation to the blueprint.

title: The 'single thesis-testing number' has no measurement channel in the design — §6 contradicts §3 and outruns ADR-0012
attack: §6 bet 1 and ADR-0009 declare 'quiz-first completion vs bypass rate' the single thesis-testing metric, and the M3 gate requires 'bypass rate & counter data flowing from ≥5 friends.' But the only instrumentation the blueprint specifies (§3 usage-counter row, per ADR-0012) is an invocation self-tally — 'self-tally line in ledger doc' on the novice tier. Bypass events are a different, finer-grained signal that no section, ADR, or component defines. Worse, on the novice tier — where M1 launches and most friends live — the tally is written by the model itself into a Project doc, with no guarantee it executes on every quiz interaction; nothing in the M1 gate tests its reliability. As designed, the project's core falsifiable bet (the conversion hypothesis, ADR-0009's revisit trigger) cannot produce a trustworthy number, and the failure will be silent: missing tallies are indistinguishable from low usage.
proposed_fix: Specify bypass-event logging in the ledger schema at M1 (each quiz session block records items offered / completed / bypassed), add 'tally reliably written across N test sessions' to the M1 item-quality gate, and amend the §3 counter row (and ADR-0012 wording if needed) to name bypass events explicitly as a counted signal.

title: Latimier expanding-schedule null stated unconditionally; the verifier's own caveat is dropped
attack: §2 rule 3: 'expanding schedules showed no advantage (g=0.034 ns, Latimier 2021, verified); don't over-engineer v1.' The g and verification status match findings.json, but the verified record's corrected_claim explicitly softens the null: 'expanding schedules became relatively more beneficial as the number of test exposures increased — so the null result does not straightforwardly condemn adaptive, many-repetition systems like Anki.' Uniform spacing is the right v1 call, but the blueprint cites the null as settled, and a semester of quiz sessions (M3/M4 regime) is exactly the many-exposure condition where the null erodes.
proposed_fix: Keep uniform spacing for v1 but carry the caveat: 'uniform spacing for v1 (expanding showed no average advantage, g=0.034 ns, Latimier verified) — revisit at M3 once users accumulate many exposures, where the same meta shows expanding schedules gain value.'

title: Fleckenstein citation: unverified status unlabeled, '1-2 sessions' relabeled 'one-off', and the revision-task null ignored
attack: §4D cites 'g=0.66 sustained vs 0.18 one-off, Fleckenstein 2023' with no status tag. findings.json shows verdict: null (never adversarially verified) and the memo files writing-feedback effect sizes under 'moderate confidence.' Two content slips: (a) the 0.18 arm is '1-2 sessions,' not strictly one-off; (b) the same meta-analysis found revision-task effects of g=0.27 and non-significant, vs transfer tasks g=0.65 — i.e., sustained automated feedback improves the *next* piece of writing, not iterative polishing of the same draft. 'Designed for repeat use across drafts' leans toward the framing the meta fails to support. The blueprint also leaves an unused favorable fact on the table: L2 writers g=0.72 vs L1 g=0.40 — directly relevant to a Romanian cohort writing English essays.
proposed_fix: Tag the citation unverified-strong; change '0.18 one-off' to '0.18 for 1-2 sessions'; reframe /feedback's goal and its eventual success measure as cross-assignment improvement ('your next essay'), and add the L2 subgroup (g=0.72) as cohort-specific support.

title: Systematic verification-status stripping: 'the verified evidence base', bare unverified citations, GAPs-as-fact, and '90%' without its tilde
attack: The blueprint header calls its input 'the verified evidence base,' but that base's own front page says only 15 of 84 findings were adversarially verified and 36 strong findings 'pass through flagged unverified.' The memo's discipline of italic status tags is dropped exactly at the document that drives the build: Bisra g=0.55 is rated *moderate* strength, verdict null (§4B cites it bare); Gollwitzer & Sheeran d=0.65 is unverified and from general goal-pursuit literature, with the memo itself listing 'planning-feature efficacy inside AI assistants is untested' as missing evidence (§4C cites it bare); Yeomans & Reich is moderate/unverified. §1 asserts 'the one thing no incumbent does (evidence GAPs 1–3)' as fact although ADR-0009 demotes the gaps to an unverified mid-2025 snapshot — §6 bet 4 hedges correctly, so §1 contradicts §6 in register. And §1's '90%, OQ-7' hardens the owner's '~90%' estimate (no N, self-report about friends) into the factual premise 'our cohort already pays for Claude Pro.'
proposed_fix: Adopt the memo's status-tag convention throughout the blueprint (verified / verified-adjacent / unverified-strong / moderate / owner-report); rename the input to 'the evidence base (15 verified load-bearing claims)'; hedge §1's GAP sentence to match §6 ('no incumbent does today — bet 4'); restore '~90% (owner estimate, OQ-7)' and confirm each friend's actual plan during the owed friend-story collection, which costs one extra question.
